name: Seven Heavens
description: The Seven Heavens, in Jewish mythology, represent a layered cosmology, a hierarchical structure of spiritual realms above the earthly plane. Each heaven is distinct, possessing its own unique characteristics, inhabitants, and degree of proximity to God. The concept is found in various Jewish texts, including the Talmud, Kabbalah, and apocalyptic literature, although the specific descriptions and functions of each heaven can vary. They are generally envisioned as progressively more spiritual and ethereal, ascending in holiness and divine presence. The highest heaven is often considered the dwelling place of God and the most elevated angels. The journey through the Seven Heavens can be interpreted as a mystical ascent, a spiritual progression towards divine union. They serve as a symbol of the complexity and grandeur of the divine realm and the potential for spiritual growth and enlightenment. The idea provides a framework for understanding the relationship between the earthly and the divine, and the different levels of spiritual experience.
subtitle: Layered cosmology of spiritual realms.

keyMyths.title: Enoch's Ascent
keyMyths.description: Enoch's journey through the Seven Heavens is described in some texts, where he ascends to receive divine knowledge and becomes the angel Metatron. This journey highlights the possibility of human ascent to the divine realms.

keyMyths.title: The Vision of Isaiah
keyMyths.description: Isaiah's prophetic vision includes glimpses of the heavens and the divine throne, suggesting a hierarchical structure of spiritual realms. This reinforces the concept of layered heavens and their significance in divine revelation.

sources.author: Gershom Scholem
sources.description: Discusses the Kabbalistic interpretations of the Seven Heavens.
sources.text: Discusses the Kabbalistic interpretations of the Seven Heavens.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: Contains various references to the concept of multiple heavens.
sources.text: Contains various references to the concept of multiple heavens.
